text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY — SURVEILLANCE VISIT NOTE

ATTENDING: [Head and Neck Medical Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Head and Neck Oncology / Otolaryngology Collaborative Clinic
VISIT TYPE: Scheduled Surveillance — 24-Month Post-Treatment Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Routine 2-year post-treatment surveillance visit. Patient presents for scheduled follow-up following curative-intent concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Margaret Chen is a 54-year-old East Asian woman and never-smoker with a history of Stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive staging) moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, p16 strongly positive, HPV-16 confirmed by in situ hybridization. She was treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation approximately 2 years ago: intensity-modulated radiation therapy to 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the primary and gross nodal disease, with concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 every 3 weeks. She received 2 of 3 planned cycles of cisplatin (cumulative dose 340 mg/m2); the third cycle was held due to nephrotoxicity (creatinine peaked at 1.32 mg/dL during week 6 of treatment). Her acute treatment course was complicated by grade 3 mucositis requiring PEG tube placement for nutritional support, grade 2 radiation dermatitis with moist desquamation, and grade 1 peripheral neuropathy in the fingertips. She achieved a complete metabolic response on 12-week post-treatment PET-CT. PEG tube was successfully removed approximately 4 months after treatment completion.

Subsequent surveillance has been reassuring throughout. Six-month surveillance CT neck showed no evidence of recurrence, with the previously enlarged left level IIA node fully resolved. Twelve-month PET-CT was negative for FDG-avid disease at the primary site, regional nodes, or distant sites. Eighteen-month flexible nasopharyngoscopy was negative. Notably, she developed post-radiation hypothyroidism, confirmed at the 12-month visit (TSH 7.1, free T4 0.8 ng/dL), and was initiated on levothyroxine 50 mcg, subsequently uptitrated to 75 mcg with normalization of TSH to 1.9 mIU/L at the 18-month visit.

She presents today for her scheduled 2-year comprehensive surveillance evaluation. She reports feeling well overall. She has no new complaints referable to the head and neck. She specifically denies:
- New throat pain or odynophagia
- Return of the globus sensation that originally prompted her workup
- Hoarseness or voice change
- Otalgia or new ear fullness
- Hemoptysis or blood-tinged saliva
- Trismus or difficulty opening the mouth
- New neck mass or swelling
- Dyspnea
- Unexplained weight loss

She does report persistent mild xerostomia, which she describes as manageable and clearly improved compared to the first year after treatment. She uses Biotene oral spray and sips water throughout the day. She is back to teaching full-time as a high school music teacher and says she is able to project her voice adequately in the classroom. She notes that on some days, particularly when she talks extensively, her throat feels dry and slightly fatigued by the end of the day, but this does not significantly limit her activities. Swallowing is functionally near-normal; she eats a general diet without restriction. She notes residual difficulty with very dry foods such as crackers or bread without liquids, but this is a minor inconvenience. No weight loss since the 18-month visit. She has been active in a cancer survivorship program and serves as a community advocate for HPV vaccination in her school.

Her levothyroxine is being co-managed with endocrinology; she reports compliance with 75 mcg daily and no thyroid-related symptoms. She has mild tingling in her fingertips bilaterally that she attributes to the peripheral neuropathy from cisplatin; she reports it is stable and not worsening, and does not significantly interfere with her daily activities, including playing piano.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive: mild xerostomia (persistent, improved), mild bilateral fingertip tingling (stable, cisplatin-related), voice fatigue with prolonged speaking, occasional difficulty with dry foods.
Negative: no fever, chills, night sweats, significant unintentional weight change, new lymphadenopathy, odynophagia, hemoptysis, nasal obstruction or epistaxis, facial numbness, trismus, dyspnea, chest pain, cough, bone pain, or neurological symptoms. No depression or anxiety reported; patient describes mood as positive and engaged.

---

MEDICATIONS
1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally once daily (post-radiation hypothyroidism)
2. Biotene oral spray as needed for xerostomia
3. Ibuprofen 400 mg as needed for occasional muscle aches (infrequent use)

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no distress. Alert and conversant. Appears her stated age.
Vital Signs: Weight 58 kg (stable from 18-month visit; 4 kg below pre-treatment baseline of 62 kg). BP 118/74 mmHg. HR 72 bpm. RR 16. SpO2 98% on room air.
Performance Status: ECOG 0.

Head/Face/Oral Cavity: No trismus. Mouth opening adequate. Oral mucosa appears moist with mild residual radiation-related mucosal changes; no ulcerations, no leukoplakia, no erythematous lesions, no masses. Dentition intact. Palate symmetrical. Tongue mobile with full range of motion.

Oropharynx / Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy (performed at today's visit): Procedure well tolerated. Nasal passages patent bilaterally. Nasopharynx without lesion or adenoid hypertrophy. Soft palate and uvula symmetric. Left base of tongue mucosa appears smooth and healed without raised, friable, or ulcerative lesions. No asymmetric fullness or submucosal bulge to suggest submucosal recurrence. Vallecula clear. Epiglottis normal contour. Hypopharynx and postcricoid region unremarkable. True and false vocal cords symmetric with normal mobility bilaterally. Subglottis not visualized. No lesions detected.

Neck: Supple. Mild fibrosis of the bilateral anterior neck skin consistent with radiation changes, stable in appearance. No new skin changes, no radiation-induced telangiectasias of note. No palpable cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Left level II region palpated carefully — no discrete nodularity or firmness. Thyroid not palpably enlarged.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II–XII grossly intact. Mild symmetric distal fingertip dysesthesias noted on sensory examination, consistent with grade 1 cisplatin-associated peripheral neuropathy, unchanged from prior visits.

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly. PEG tube site well healed, no residual abnormality.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (drawn at today's visit)
TSH: 2.4 mIU/L — within normal range, well controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg
Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL — normal
CBC: WBC 5.8 x10^9/L, Hgb 13.2 g/dL, Plt 228 x10^9/L — all within normal limits
BMP: Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Creatinine 0.79 mg/dL (at or below pre-treatment baseline of 0.78), BUN 14 mg/dL — normal
Magnesium: 2.0 mEq/L — normal (had required repletion during treatment)
Albumin: 4.0 g/dL — normal

---

IMAGING
CT neck with contrast ordered at today's visit (results pending; to be reviewed at next appointment or by phone/portal). This will constitute the 24-month post-treatment imaging surveillance evaluation. PET-CT was last performed at 12 months and was completely negative. Per current practice for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC following complete response, CT neck is the preferred surveillance modality at this interval in the absence of clinical concern.

Most recent relevant imaging: PET-CT at 12 months post-treatment — no FDG-avid disease at primary site, regional nodes, or distant sites. CT neck at 6 months — no recurrent malignancy, left level IIA node resolved to 0.7 cm within normal limits.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, Stage III (T2N1M0) — 2-year surveillance, no evidence of disease.
Ms. Chen presents for her 2-year comprehensive post-treatment evaluation following definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT. She has achieved and maintained a durable complete response. Today's history, physical examination, and flexible nasopharyngoscopy are all reassuring with no clinical evidence of locoregional recurrence. This is a highly favorable milestone for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC; the vast majority of recurrences in this disease — which remain uncommon given her favorable biology — occur within the first 2 years, and she is now at the 2-year mark without any sign of recurrence. The risk of late relapse beyond this point is low, though surveillance will continue.

Imaging: CT neck with contrast ordered as the 2-year surveillance scan. Results to be reviewed promptly. Will contact the patient by phone with results once available, or she can review via the patient portal. If there is any finding of concern, she will be brought back for urgent evaluation.

Surveillance going forward: Clinical examination with flexible nasopharyngoscopy every 6 months through year 3, then annually thereafter per institutional protocol for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC. Imaging will be obtained as clinically indicated; no routine scheduled PET-CT planned at this time given sustained complete response and favorable disease biology.

2. Post-radiation hypothyroidism — well controlled.
TSH today 2.4 mIU/L, free T4 1.1 ng/dL — excellent control on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. No dose adjustment needed. Will continue endocrinology co-management with annual TSH monitoring.

3. Cisplatin-related peripheral neuropathy — stable, grade 1.
Mild bilateral fingertip dysesthesias, stable and non-progressive over the past year. No functional limitation reported. Given stable course and 2+ years since cisplatin exposure, significant further improvement is less likely but progression also not anticipated. Patient is accepting of current symptom level. No intervention needed at this time; will monitor at future visits.

4. Xerostomia — persistent grade 1, improved.
Continue Biotene spray and hydration strategies. Discussed dental hygiene vigilance given ongoing xerostomia and associated risk of radiation caries; encouraged continued twice-daily fluoride toothpaste use and dental follow-up every 6 months with a provider familiar with her radiation history. Will also inquire about pilocarpine if xerostomia worsens, though current level is mild.

5. Swallowing and nutrition — stable, functional.
Weight stable at 58 kg, BMI approximately 21.9 kg/m2. Near-normal diet tolerated. No further speech-language pathology intervention indicated at this time unless she develops new swallowing symptoms.

6. Cancer survivorship and psychosocial.
Patient is doing remarkably well. She has returned to full-time work and is an active HPV vaccination advocate in her school community. She was connected with survivorship program resources and continues to engage with them. Discussion today reinforced the very favorable long-term prognosis of HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC in never-smokers achieving complete response to standard therapy. Patient is informed and engaged; questions were answered.

Return: Follow-up appointment to be scheduled in approximately 6 months (30-month mark) for clinical examination and nasopharyngoscopy. CT neck imaging results to be communicated promptly.

Time spent in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 35 minutes.
